title: Microsoft Fabric for Data Engineers: Mid-Level Track
description: Performance optimization, Delta Lake operations, advanced pipeline patterns, and production-grade Fabric practices for experienced data engineers.

chapters.title: Spark Performance in Fabric Notebooks
chapters.description: Read and interpret execution plans in Fabric's Spark environment, tune partition strategies for Lakehouse workloads, and use the Spark UI to diagnose performance problems before they reach production.

blocks.content: In the junior-level track, NordGrid processed 500,000 meter readings per day across 4 regions. The Silver transformation notebook ran in 12 minutes. The pipeline completed before 02:30 UTC. Nobody checked the Spark UI because nothing was slow. That changes now. NordGrid has expanded to 1.2 million meters across 12 regions, and the same notebook — the same code, the same logic, the same saveAsTable call — takes 47 minutes. The pipeline misses its 03:00 UTC SLA. The operations dashboard shows stale data at 08:00. The cause is not a bug. It is a performance problem that only manifests at scale, and solving it requires skills the junior-level track did not teach.
The Junior-Level Assumption

At the junior level, you trusted Spark's defaults. You wrote df.filter(...).groupBy(...).saveAsTable(...) and Spark figured out the rest — how many partitions to use, whether to broadcast a small table, how to order the operations. That trust was well-placed at 500K rows. Spark's Catalyst optimizer makes reasonable default choices for small-to-medium workloads. But defaults are tuned for generality, not for your specific data. At 1.2M rows per day with 12 regions and three-year history (1.3 billion rows), the defaults produce a plan that shuffles 3.2 GB across 200 partitions when it should shuffle 400 MB across 48 partitions. The gap between the default plan and the optimal plan is where mid-level performance tuning lives.
The Measure-Diagnose-Fix-Verify Cycle

Mid-level performance tuning is not guesswork. It follows a four-step cycle. Measure: establish a baseline by recording the notebook's duration, the shuffle volume, and the stage breakdown. Diagnose: read the execution plan and the Spark UI to identify the bottleneck — is it shuffle, scan, skew, or spill? Fix: apply a targeted intervention — adjust partition count, add a filter, change a join strategy. Verify: rerun and compare to the baseline. If the numbers improved, the fix worked. If not, the diagnosis was wrong, and you iterate. NordGrid's mid-level team never applies a performance fix without measuring before and after.

import time

# Measure: record baseline before any optimization
start = time.time()

df_silver = (
    spark.read.csv(input_path, header=True, schema=meter_schema)
    .filter(col("meter_id").isNotNull())
    .filter(col("daily_kw") > 0)
    .filter(col("daily_kw") < 10000)
    .dropDuplicates(["meter_id", "date"])
    .withColumn("year", year(col("date")))
    .withColumn("month", month(col("date")))
)

df_silver.write.mode("overwrite").partitionBy("year", "month").saveAsTable("silver_meters")

elapsed = time.time() - start
print(f"Baseline: {elapsed:.0f} seconds")
# Output: Baseline: 2820 seconds (47 minutes)

The baseline tells you where you start. Forty-seven minutes. The SLA is 20 minutes (to allow the Gold notebook to run before 03:00 UTC). The gap is 27 minutes. The next step is diagnosis: where are those 47 minutes being spent? This module teaches the tools — execution plans (lessons 2–4), partition tuning (lesson 5), the Spark UI (lesson 6), and AQE (lesson 7) — and the diagnostic workflows (lesson 8) that answer that question. By the end of this module, you will reduce NordGrid's Silver notebook from 47 minutes to under 15.
The Three Bottleneck Categories

rows: Too much data moves between executors during a repartition, join, or groupBy
Spark UI shows large shuffle read/write volumes; Exchange nodes in the execution plan
Reduce partition count, pre-filter before shuffle, broadcast small tables
Spark reads more data from OneLake than necessary — missing partition pruning or column pruning
Spark UI shows large input size; execution plan shows no PushedFilters or no PartitionFilters
Add partition filters, use select() to prune columns, verify pushdown in the plan
One partition has far more data than others, causing one task to run much longer than the rest
Spark UI shows one task with 10x+ duration compared to the median; uneven executor utilization
Salt the skewed key, enable AQE skew handling, repartition with a better key

blocks.title: Never optimize without measuring first
blocks.content: The most expensive performance mistake is not a slow pipeline — it is a fast pipeline that was optimized for the wrong bottleneck. NordGrid's team once spent two days tuning partition counts to reduce shuffle, only to discover that the actual bottleneck was a full table scan caused by a missing date filter. The partition tuning made no difference because the scan consumed 80% of the runtime. Measure first. Diagnose the actual bottleneck. Then fix it. The measure-diagnose-fix-verify cycle is not bureaucracy — it is the difference between solving the real problem and wasting time on the wrong one.

An execution plan is Spark's internal representation of what it will do with your code. When you write df.filter(...).groupBy(...).agg(...), Spark does not execute those operations directly. It translates your code into a plan — a tree of operators — and then optimizes that plan through the Catalyst optimizer before executing it. Reading execution plans is the single most important diagnostic skill at the mid-level. The plan tells you exactly what Spark will do: which files it will scan, which columns it will read, where it will shuffle, and which join strategy it will use. If the plan is wrong, the performance is wrong — and no amount of cluster tuning will fix a bad plan.
The explain() Method

In a Fabric notebook, calling explain() on any DataFrame prints its physical execution plan — the sequence of operators Spark will execute. Calling explain(True) prints all four plan phases: Parsed Logical Plan, Analyzed Logical Plan, Optimized Logical Plan, and Physical Plan. The Physical Plan is what matters for performance — it shows the actual operators Spark will execute, including scan strategies, shuffle boundaries, and join types. The other three phases are useful for understanding how Catalyst transformed your code, but the physical plan is where you find the bottlenecks.

# Read the Silver table and compute a regional summary
df = spark.read.table("silver_meters")
df_summary = (
    df.filter(col("year") == 2025)
      .filter(col("month") == 3)
      .groupBy("region")
      .agg(
          count("*").alias("meter_count"),
          round(sum("daily_kw"), 2).alias("total_kw"),
          round(avg("daily_kw"), 2).alias("avg_kw")
      )
)

# Print only the physical plan
df_summary.explain()

# Print all four plan phases
df_summary.explain(True)
Reading the Physical Plan Bottom-Up

Physical plans read bottom-up. The lowest operator is where execution starts (typically a file scan), and the topmost operator is where execution ends (typically a projection or aggregate). Each operator consumes the output of the operator below it. Indentation shows the tree structure: deeply indented operators execute first, and their results flow upward to less-indented operators. This bottom-up reading is counterintuitive at first — you write code top-down (filter, then group, then aggregate) but read the plan bottom-up (scan, then filter, then exchange, then aggregate). NordGrid's engineers read every plan from the bottom FileScan upward, tracing the data through each operator.

== Physical Plan ==
AdaptiveSparkPlan isFinalPlan=false
+- HashAggregate(keys=[region#45], functions=[count(1), sum(daily_kw#46), avg(daily_kw#46)])
   +- Exchange hashpartitioning(region#45, 200), ENSURE_REQUIREMENTS, [plan_id=42]
      +- HashAggregate(keys=[region#45], functions=[partial_count(1), partial_sum(daily_kw#46), partial_avg(daily_kw#46)])
         +- Project [region#45, daily_kw#46]
            +- Filter ((year#50 = 2025) AND (month#51 = 3))
               +- FileScan delta [daily_kw#46, region#45, year#50, month#51]
                     ReadSchema: struct<daily_kw:double,region:string>
                     PartitionFilters: [isnotnull(year#50), isnotnull(month#51), (year#50 = 2025), (month#51 = 3)]
                     PushedFilters: []

Read this plan from the bottom. FileScan delta: Spark reads from the silver_meters Delta table. The PartitionFilters line shows that the year=2025 and month=3 filters are pushed into partition pruning — Spark will only read the 2025/03 partition directory, not the entire table. ReadSchema shows that only daily_kw and region are read — Spark pruned the other columns. Filter applies any remaining conditions. Project selects the needed columns. HashAggregate computes the partial aggregation (one per partition). Exchange shuffles the partial results by region. The final HashAggregate combines the partial results into the final output.
The Operator Vocabulary

rows: Reads data from OneLake Delta files
Check PartitionFilters and PushedFilters — missing filters mean full scan
Applies conditions that could not be pushed into the scan
Should be minimal — most filters should be in PartitionFilters
Selects columns needed by downstream operators
Check ReadSchema — fewer columns mean less I/O
The most expensive operator — check partition count and data volume
Appears twice: partial (before shuffle) and final (after shuffle)
Joins by broadcasting the smaller table to all executors
Good — no shuffle. Check that the broadcast table is actually small
Joins by shuffling both tables, sorting, and merging
Expensive — two shuffles. Check if broadcast is possible instead
Check if the sort is necessary — orderBy triggers a sort + exchange

blocks.title: The plan never lies
blocks.content: Your code describes what you want. The plan describes what Spark will actually do. They are often different. A filter you wrote after a join might be pushed before the join in the plan (good — fewer rows to join). A broadcast hint you added might be ignored because the table is too large (bad — sort-merge join instead). The plan is the truth. When performance does not match your expectations, read the plan first. The answer is always there.

NordGrid's Silver table contains 1.3 billion rows across three years. A query that filters to March 2025 should read approximately 15 million rows (one month). If Spark reads all 1.3 billion rows and then filters, the query takes 8 minutes. If Spark reads only the March 2025 partition, the query takes 12 seconds. The difference is partition pruning — Spark's ability to skip irrelevant partitions at scan time. This lesson teaches you to verify that pruning is happening by reading the PartitionFilters and PushedFilters fields in the execution plan, and to diagnose the common scenarios where pruning silently fails.
Partition Pruning in the Plan

# Query that should benefit from partition pruning
df = spark.read.table("silver_meters")
df_march = df.filter((col("year") == 2025) & (col("month") == 3))
df_march.explain()

== Physical Plan ==
FileScan delta [...]
  PartitionFilters: [isnotnull(year#50), isnotnull(month#51), (year#50 = 2025), (month#51 = 3)]
  PushedFilters: []
  ReadSchema: struct<meter_id:string,daily_kw:double,date:date,region:string,...>

The PartitionFilters line confirms that both filters were pushed into partition pruning. Spark will read only the files in the year=2025/month=3/ directory. The PushedFilters line is empty because the filters are on partition columns — they are handled by PartitionFilters, not PushedFilters. This is the ideal scenario: the scan reads the minimum amount of data. At NordGrid's scale, this pruning reduces the scan from 3.2 TB (full table) to approximately 90 GB (one month), a 35x reduction that directly translates to a 35x reduction in scan time.
When Pruning Fails

NordGrid's production incident: a data engineer wrote a filter on the date column instead of the year and month partition columns. The query filtered correctly — it returned only March 2025 rows — but Spark scanned the entire table because date is not a partition column. The execution plan showed PartitionFilters: [] (empty) and PushedFilters: [isnotnull(date#47), (date#47 >= 2025-03-01), (date#47 <= 2025-03-31)]. The filter was pushed as a PushedFilter (applied during scan but after reading each file), not as a PartitionFilter (applied before selecting which files to read). The query returned correct results in 8 minutes instead of 12 seconds.

# BAD: filters on non-partition column — no partition pruning
df_bad = df.filter(
    (col("date") >= "2025-03-01") & (col("date") <= "2025-03-31")
)
df_bad.explain()
# PartitionFilters: []  ← EMPTY — Spark scans ALL partitions
# PushedFilters: [IsNotNull(date), GreaterThanOrEqual(date,2025-03-01), ...]

# GOOD: filters on partition columns — partition pruning active
df_good = df.filter(
    (col("year") == 2025) & (col("month") == 3)
)
df_good.explain()
# PartitionFilters: [(year = 2025), (month = 3)]  ← Spark reads one partition

The fix is straightforward: filter on partition columns (year, month) instead of — or in addition to — the non-partition date column. If your query logic requires a precise date range (March 15 to March 20), combine both: filter on the partition columns to activate pruning, then filter on the date column to narrow further. The partition filter reduces the scan from 3.2 TB to 90 GB, and the date filter narrows the 90 GB to the exact range. Both filters are necessary — the partition filter for performance, the date filter for correctness.
Predicate Pushdown for Non-Partition Columns

Predicate pushdown is distinct from partition pruning. Partition pruning skips entire files (partition directories). Predicate pushdown applies filter conditions while reading each file, using Parquet's column statistics (min/max values per row group) to skip irrelevant row groups within a file. For example, a filter WHERE region = 'NORTH' on a non-partition column uses predicate pushdown: Spark reads each Parquet file but skips row groups where the max(region) < 'NORTH' or min(region) > 'NORTH'. Pushdown reduces the amount of data decoded, but it does not skip entire files the way pruning does.

left.label: Partition Pruning
points: Applies to partition columns (year, month in NordGrid)
Skips entire files/directories before reading any data
Shows in PartitionFilters in the execution plan
Reduction: 35x at NordGrid scale (1 month vs 36 months)
Requires table to be partitioned by the filter column

right.label: Predicate Pushdown
points: Applies to non-partition columns (region, meter_id, daily_kw)
Skips row groups within files using Parquet min/max statistics
Shows in PushedFilters in the execution plan
Reduction: 2-4x typical (depends on data distribution and statistics)
Works on any column in Parquet/Delta, no partitioning required

blocks.title: Always check PartitionFilters first
blocks.content: When diagnosing a slow scan, the first line to check in the execution plan is PartitionFilters. If it is empty and your query filters by a time range, you are scanning the entire table. This is the most common performance problem at NordGrid — and the cheapest to fix. Add the partition column filters (year, month) and the scan drops by an order of magnitude. Check this before investigating any other performance concern.

NordGrid's Silver table has 9 columns: meter_id, daily_kw, date, region, customer_type, year, month, daily_mw, and processing_timestamp. A regional summary query needs only 2 of those columns: daily_kw and region. If Spark reads all 9 columns from OneLake, it transfers 4.5x more data than necessary. At NordGrid's scale, that excess transfer adds 3 minutes to a query that should take 40 seconds. Column pruning is Spark's optimization that reads only the columns the query actually uses — and verifying that it works is a two-second check in the execution plan that saves minutes of runtime.
How Column Pruning Works

Delta tables store data in Parquet format, which is columnar: each column's values are stored contiguously, separately from other columns. When Spark needs only daily_kw and region, it reads only those two columns' data blocks from the Parquet files, skipping the other seven columns entirely. This happens at the file reader level — the data for meter_id, customer_type, and processing_timestamp is never read from OneLake. The execution plan's ReadSchema field shows exactly which columns Spark will read, making verification trivial.

# GOOD: select only the columns needed — triggers column pruning
df = spark.read.table("silver_meters")
df_summary = (
    df.filter((col("year") == 2025) & (col("month") == 3))
      .select("region", "daily_kw")       # Only 2 columns needed
      .groupBy("region")
      .agg(sum("daily_kw").alias("total_kw"))
)
df_summary.explain()

== Physical Plan ==
...
+- FileScan delta [daily_kw#46, region#45, year#50, month#51]
      ReadSchema: struct<daily_kw:double,region:string>
      PartitionFilters: [(year#50 = 2025), (month#51 = 3)]

The ReadSchema line shows struct<daily_kw:double,region:string> — only the two columns the query needs. The FileScan references year and month as well, but those are partition columns read from the directory structure, not from the Parquet data blocks. Spark pruned 7 of 9 columns automatically. Note that the explicit select('region', 'daily_kw') is not strictly necessary — Catalyst is smart enough to prune columns even without it. But the explicit select makes the pruning visible in the code, which is why NordGrid's coding standard requires it.
When Pruning Breaks

Column pruning fails when the query references all columns — intentionally or accidentally. The most common accident is using select('*') or referencing df without narrowing columns before a join or write. At junior level, this was harmless because the table was small. At 1.3 billion rows, reading 9 columns instead of 2 means reading 4.5x more data from OneLake, which directly increases I/O time. NordGrid's pipeline review checklist includes: 'verify ReadSchema in the execution plan shows only the columns the query needs.'

# BAD: no select() before join — all 9 columns are read and shuffled
df_meters = spark.read.table("silver_meters")
df_subs = spark.read.table("ref_substations")
df_joined = df_meters.join(df_subs, on="region")
# ReadSchema: struct<meter_id:string,daily_kw:double,date:date,region:string,
#   customer_type:string,year:int,month:int,daily_mw:double,processing_timestamp:timestamp>
# ALL 9 columns read and shuffled — 4.5x more data than needed

# GOOD: select() before join — only needed columns read and shuffled
df_meters = spark.read.table("silver_meters").select("region", "daily_kw", "year", "month")
df_subs = spark.read.table("ref_substations").select("region", "substation_id", "capacity_mw")
df_joined = df_meters.join(df_subs, on="region")
# ReadSchema for meters: struct<daily_kw:double,region:string>
# 4 columns instead of 9 — 2.25x less data read

blocks.title: Column pruning affects shuffle too
blocks.content: When a join or groupBy triggers a shuffle (Exchange operator), Spark shuffles all columns in the DataFrame at that point — not just the columns the final output needs. If you read all 9 columns before a shuffle, all 9 columns are shuffled. If you select 4 columns before the shuffle, only 4 columns move across the network. This means column pruning before a shuffle reduces both scan I/O and shuffle volume. At NordGrid's scale, selecting 4 columns instead of 9 before the Silver table's regional join reduced shuffle volume from 14 GB to 6 GB and cut the shuffle stage from 4 minutes to 90 seconds.

Spark's default shuffle partition count is 200 — the value of spark.sql.shuffle.partitions. At 500K rows, 200 partitions worked fine: each partition held about 2,500 rows, processed in milliseconds. At 1.2M rows per day with three years of history, 200 partitions after a groupBy on region produces 200 output partitions where most contain a few hundred rows and a few contain millions. The partition distribution is uneven, the overhead of managing 200 tasks exceeds the cost of the actual computation, and the saveAsTable call writes 200 small files instead of 12 reasonably sized ones. Partition count tuning is the simplest performance fix with the largest impact at NordGrid's scale.
The Right Partition Size

The target partition size for Spark is 128–256 MB of compressed data per partition. Smaller partitions create excessive task overhead (Spark spends more time scheduling tasks than executing them). Larger partitions risk out-of-memory errors when an executor tries to hold the entire partition in memory during a sort or aggregation. NordGrid's Silver table is approximately 90 GB for one month. At 128 MB per partition, that means approximately 700 partitions — far more than the default 200 but appropriately sized for the data volume.

# Check current partition count after a shuffle
df = spark.read.table("silver_meters").filter((col("year") == 2025) & (col("month") == 3))
df_grouped = df.groupBy("region").agg(count("*").alias("cnt"))

# Default: 200 shuffle partitions
print(f"Partitions after groupBy: {df_grouped.rdd.getNumPartitions()}")
# Output: 200

# Set shuffle partitions based on data volume
# 90 GB month / 128 MB target = ~700 partitions
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", 48)
# For a groupBy on 12 regions, 48 partitions (4 per region) is sufficient
# because the output is tiny — 12 rows, not 90 GB

df_grouped = df.groupBy("region").agg(count("*").alias("cnt"))
print(f"Partitions after tuning: {df_grouped.rdd.getNumPartitions()}")
# Output: 48

The key insight is that the right partition count depends on the data volume after the shuffle, not before it. A groupBy('region') on 15 million rows produces 12 output rows (one per region) — the output is tiny regardless of the input size. Setting 200 shuffle partitions for 12 output rows means 188 empty partitions and 12 partitions with one row each. Setting 48 is still overkill for 12 rows, but it is a reasonable default that handles both small aggregations and larger transformations in the same notebook.
Fabric-Specific Considerations

In a standalone Spark cluster, you tune partition counts based on the number of executor cores — the 2–3x cores heuristic. In Fabric, the cluster is managed by the capacity pool and the exact executor count depends on the capacity SKU and current load. NordGrid's F64 capacity typically allocates 8 executors with 4 cores each (32 cores total). Following the 2–3x heuristic, the partition count should be 64–96 for transformations where the output is large. For aggregations where the output is small, a lower count (24–48) avoids empty-partition overhead. NordGrid sets spark.sql.shuffle.partitions to 48 as the notebook default and overrides to 96 for the Silver write operation.

# NordGrid's partition strategy for the Silver transformation notebook

# Default for aggregations and small-output operations
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", 48)

# For the Silver write (large output — 15M rows per month):
# 15M rows × ~200 bytes/row ≈ 3 GB → 3 GB / 128 MB = ~24 partitions
# But the table is partitioned by year/month, so partitionBy handles distribution
# Use repartition before write only if file sizes are uneven

df_final.write \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .partitionBy("year", "month") \
    .saveAsTable("silver_meters")

# Verify: check output file count and sizes
files = spark.sql("DESCRIBE DETAIL silver_meters").select("numFiles").collect()
print(f"Output files: {files[0][0]}")

blocks.title: Let AQE handle it when possible
blocks.content: Fabric enables Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) by default. AQE's coalesceShufflePartitions feature automatically merges small shuffle partitions at runtime, which means setting spark.sql.shuffle.partitions to a moderately high value (like 200) and letting AQE reduce it is often sufficient. However, AQE cannot increase partitions — if you set the count too low and one partition is too large, AQE cannot split it. NordGrid's approach: set a reasonable default (48–96) and let AQE fine-tune downward. Lesson 7 covers AQE in depth.

The execution plan tells you what Spark will do. The Spark UI tells you what Spark actually did — the runtime metrics, the actual data volumes, the task durations, and the resource utilization. When a notebook is slow and the plan looks reasonable, the Spark UI reveals the runtime truth: a skewed partition where one task took 20 minutes while the other 47 finished in 30 seconds, an executor that ran out of memory and spilled to disk, or a shuffle that moved 14 GB when the plan estimated 2 GB. This lesson teaches how to access the Spark UI in Fabric and how to read the four tabs that matter most for performance diagnosis.
Accessing the Spark UI

In Fabric, the Spark UI is accessible from two places. During an active notebook session, click the Spark jobs link in the notebook's status bar (at the bottom of the editor). After a notebook run completes, open the monitoring hub, find the notebook run, and click the Spark UI link in the run details. NordGrid's engineers use the second path for post-run analysis: the notebook ran overnight, and they review the Spark UI the next morning to check for performance regressions.
The Jobs Tab

The Jobs tab shows every Spark job triggered during the notebook run. Each action (count(), show(), saveAsTable()) triggers a separate job. The tab displays each job's duration, the number of stages, and the number of tasks. NordGrid's Silver notebook triggers four jobs: the Bronze CSV read (job 0), the count after filtering (job 1), the saveAsTable write (job 2), and the validation query (job 3). The Jobs tab immediately shows which job consumed the most time — at NordGrid, job 2 (the write) takes 60% of the total duration.
The Stages Tab

Clicking into a job reveals its stages. A stage is a unit of work that runs without a shuffle — every shuffle boundary creates a new stage. The Stages tab shows each stage's input size, output size, shuffle read/write volumes, and the task duration distribution (min, median, max, 75th percentile). The task duration distribution is the most important metric for detecting skew: if the max task duration is 10x the median, one partition is doing far more work than the others. NordGrid's daily pipeline had a stage where the median task took 8 seconds and the max took 4 minutes — a classic skew signal that AQE resolved once diagnosed.

blocks.title: Spark UI drill-down: Jobs → Stages → Tasks
blocks.content:
JOBS TAB
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Job 0: Read CSV          │ 2 min  │ 1 stg│
│ Job 1: Count             │ 30 sec │ 1 stg│
│ Job 2: SaveAsTable       │ 28 min │ 3 stg│ ← bottleneck
│ Job 3: Validation query  │ 15 sec │ 1 stg│
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
              │
              ▼  Click Job 2
STAGES TAB
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Stage 4: Scan + Filter   │ 3 min  │ 48 tasks │
│ Stage 5: Shuffle (Exch)  │ 18 min │ 48 tasks │ ← bottleneck
│ Stage 6: Write           │ 7 min  │ 12 tasks │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
              │
              ▼  Click Stage 5
TASKS
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Min: 4 sec │ Median: 12 sec │ Max: 16 min│
│ Shuffle Read: 14 GB total                │
│ Spill (disk): 2.1 GB                    │ ← memory pressure
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
blocks.caption: The Spark UI reveals the bottleneck through progressive drill-down. Jobs show which action is slow. Stages show which stage within that job is slow. Tasks show whether the slowness is uniform (all tasks slow — too much data) or skewed (one task slow — data skew).

blocks.content: The SQL/DataFrame Tab

The SQL/DataFrame tab shows the execution plan as a visual DAG with actual metrics. Each operator node displays the actual number of rows processed, the actual data size, and the actual time spent. This is more useful than explain() because explain() shows estimates while the SQL tab shows actuals. NordGrid's engineers compare the estimated row count from the plan with the actual row count from the SQL tab — a large discrepancy indicates stale statistics that caused Catalyst to make a suboptimal plan choice.

blocks.title: The Spark UI is the performance debugger
blocks.content: The execution plan is the performance specification — it shows what Spark intends to do. The Spark UI is the performance debugger — it shows what Spark actually did, how long each piece took, and where the bottleneck hides. NordGrid's rule: never file a performance complaint without a Spark UI screenshot showing the bottleneck. 'The notebook is slow' is not a diagnosis. 'Stage 5 takes 18 minutes because the shuffle reads 14 GB across 48 partitions with 2.1 GB of disk spill' is a diagnosis that leads directly to a fix.

Catalyst optimizes the execution plan before the query runs, using table statistics and heuristics. But Catalyst's estimates are sometimes wrong — the table statistics are stale, the data distribution has changed, or the filter selectivity is different from what Catalyst predicted. Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) is a runtime optimizer that adjusts the plan during execution based on actual intermediate results. In Fabric, AQE is enabled by default, and its three features — partition coalescing, join conversion, and skew handling — address the three most common mid-level performance problems automatically.
Feature 1: Coalescing Shuffle Partitions

When spark.sql.shuffle.partitions is set to 200 but the actual shuffle output is small (say, 12 rows for a regional groupBy), AQE merges the 200 partitions down to a smaller number at runtime. This eliminates the overhead of scheduling and coordinating 188 empty tasks. You can see this in the Spark UI: the plan shows Exchange with 200 partitions, but the actual execution shows CustomShuffleReader with a reduced partition count. NordGrid's regional aggregation goes from 200 planned partitions to 8 actual partitions after AQE coalescing.
Feature 2: Converting Sort-Merge to Broadcast Join

Catalyst decides the join strategy before execution based on estimated table sizes. If both tables appear large, Catalyst chooses a sort-merge join (two shuffles). But if one table is actually small after filters are applied (the estimate was wrong because statistics were stale), AQE can convert the sort-merge join to a broadcast join at runtime — eliminating both shuffles. NordGrid's enrichment join between filtered Silver meters (15M rows for one month) and the substations reference table (200 rows) was planned as sort-merge because Catalyst estimated the Silver filter would return 500M rows. AQE saw the actual 15M rows and converted to broadcast.

# AQE will convert this sort-merge join to broadcast at runtime
# if the filtered Silver table is small enough after filter evaluation
df_meters = (
    spark.read.table("silver_meters")
    .filter((col("year") == 2025) & (col("month") == 3))
    .select("region", "daily_kw")
)
df_subs = spark.read.table("ref_substations")

df_joined = df_meters.join(df_subs, on="region")
df_joined.explain()
# Plan shows SortMergeJoin (Catalyst's pre-execution choice)
# But actual execution uses BroadcastHashJoin (AQE's runtime optimization)
# Verify in the Spark UI SQL tab: look for 'AQE' annotations on join nodes
Feature 3: Skew Join Optimization

When AQE detects that one partition in a shuffle is significantly larger than others (skew), it splits that partition into smaller sub-partitions and replicates the corresponding partition from the other side of the join to match. This eliminates the single-task bottleneck without requiring manual salting. NordGrid's NORTH-1 region contains 25% of all meters (300K of 1.2M) while other sub-regions average 75K. Without AQE skew handling, the NORTH-1 partition takes 4x longer than any other. With AQE, the NORTH-1 partition is split into 4 sub-partitions that complete in the same time as the others.
Verifying AQE in the Spark UI

The Spark UI's SQL/DataFrame tab shows AQE activity with specific annotations. Look for CustomShuffleReader (coalescing happened), AQEShuffleRead (a shuffle was optimized), and the isFinalPlan=true marker at the top of the plan (AQE has finalized the plan after observing actual statistics). If isFinalPlan=false, AQE has not yet run — the plan is the pre-execution estimate. NordGrid's engineers verify AQE behavior after every pipeline run by checking the SQL tab for these markers. If AQE is not activating when expected, the cause is usually a configuration override that disabled it.

blocks.title: AQE is a safety net, not a strategy
blocks.content: AQE fixes problems that Catalyst could not predict. It does not replace good data engineering practices. A notebook that relies on AQE to coalesce 200 partitions down to 8 every single run is wasting compute on the initial 200-partition shuffle before AQE intervenes. A notebook that sets partition count to 48 from the start avoids the waste entirely. Use AQE as a safety net for unexpected conditions (data volume spikes, stale statistics) and good configuration as the baseline. NordGrid's approach: configure well, let AQE handle the exceptions.

Lessons 2–7 taught the individual tools: execution plans, partition pruning, column pruning, partition count tuning, the Spark UI, and AQE. This lesson combines them into a diagnostic workflow — the step-by-step process NordGrid's engineers follow when a notebook exceeds its SLA. The workflow is designed to find the bottleneck in the minimum number of steps, starting with the cheapest checks (cold start separation, job-level timing) and progressing to deeper investigation (stage metrics, task distribution, plan analysis) only when the cheap checks do not reveal the cause.
The Diagnostic Workflow

items: Separate cold start from execution. Check the notebook's total run time in the monitoring hub. Subtract the Spark session cold start time (typically 30–60 seconds in Fabric). If the cold start is 60 seconds and the total is 180 seconds, the actual computation is only 120 seconds — the problem might be cold start, not the query.

Identify the slowest job. Open the Spark UI Jobs tab. Find the job with the longest duration. In NordGrid's Silver notebook, job 2 (saveAsTable) is always the longest. If job 0 (read) is the longest, the bottleneck is I/O, not computation.
Identify the slowest stage within that job. Click into the job and find the stage with the longest duration. Check the stage's shuffle read/write volumes, input size, and task count.

Check the task duration distribution. If max/median > 5x, the problem is skew — one partition is doing most of the work. If max/median ≈ 1x and all tasks are uniformly slow, the problem is volume — too much data per task.
Check the execution plan. Run explain() on the DataFrame. Verify PartitionFilters (partition pruning active?), ReadSchema (column pruning active?), and join strategy (broadcast or sort-merge?).

Apply the fix based on the diagnosis. Skew → enable AQE skew handling or salt the key. Volume → reduce scan via pruning or increase parallelism. Shuffle → reduce partition count or broadcast small tables. Full scan → add partition column filters.

blocks.content: NordGrid's 47-Minute Notebook: The Diagnosis

Applying the workflow to NordGrid's slow Silver notebook: Step 1: Total time 47 minutes, cold start 45 seconds — the computation is 46 minutes. Step 2: Job 2 (saveAsTable) takes 40 of 46 minutes. Step 3: Stage 5 (the shuffle before the write) takes 28 minutes. The shuffle reads 14 GB. Step 4: Task duration distribution shows max 16 minutes, median 12 seconds — extreme skew. Step 5: The execution plan shows Exchange hashpartitioning(region, 200) — the shuffle partitions by region with 200 partitions. Diagnosis: the NORTH-1 region contains 25% of the data, creating a single massive partition.
The Fix

# Before: shuffle by region with 200 partitions — NORTH-1 gets 25% of data
# Duration: 47 minutes

# Fix 1: Reduce shuffle partitions to match actual parallelism
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", 48)

# Fix 2: Enable AQE skew handling (already enabled by default in Fabric)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled", "true")

# Fix 3: Add column pruning before the write
df_final = (
    df_clean
    .select("meter_id", "daily_kw", "date", "region", "customer_type",
            "year", "month", "daily_mw", "processing_timestamp")
)

# After: 14 minutes (from 47)
# Breakdown: cold start 45s, read 2min, transform 3min, write 8min, validate 30s

Three fixes combined reduced the notebook from 47 minutes to 14 minutes — a 3.4x improvement that brought it well within the 20-minute SLA. The largest contributor was the partition count reduction (200 to 48) combined with AQE skew handling, which eliminated the 16-minute outlier task. The column pruning contributed a smaller but meaningful reduction by cutting shuffle volume from 14 GB to 6 GB. Total investigation time: 20 minutes. Total fix implementation time: 5 minutes. The measure-diagnose-fix-verify cycle worked.

blocks.title: Most slow notebooks have one bottleneck
blocks.content: In NordGrid's experience, 80% of slow notebooks have a single dominant bottleneck that accounts for 60–80% of the total runtime. Finding that one bottleneck and fixing it provides the majority of the improvement. The diagnostic workflow is designed to find that bottleneck quickly — the first four steps take 5 minutes and identify the problem in most cases. Resist the temptation to apply every optimization technique simultaneously. Fix the biggest bottleneck, measure the result, and decide whether further optimization is needed.

Lessons 2–8 covered Spark performance techniques that apply in any Spark environment — execution plans, pruning, partition tuning, the Spark UI, and AQE. This lesson covers performance patterns that are unique to Fabric's managed Spark environment: capacity throttling that slows notebooks during peak usage, V-Order optimization that trades write speed for read speed, session pool management that eliminates cold start overhead, and the interaction between Spark notebooks and other Fabric workloads sharing the same capacity. These patterns have no equivalent in standalone Spark clusters because they arise from Fabric's SaaS architecture.
Capacity Throttling

Fabric's capacity model allocates compute resources from a shared pool. When the pool is heavily utilized — multiple notebooks running simultaneously, a large Power BI refresh in progress, a Warehouse query consuming significant resources — Fabric throttles lower-priority workloads by queuing them or reducing their executor allocation. NordGrid's production incident: the Silver notebook normally completes in 14 minutes, but on the first Monday of the month it took 38 minutes. The cause was the monthly billing Power BI report refresh running simultaneously, consuming 40% of the F64 capacity. The notebook received fewer executors and each task took longer.

The fix is scheduling discipline: NordGrid staggers the Silver notebook (02:00 UTC), the Gold notebook (02:20 UTC), and the Power BI refresh (04:00 UTC) to avoid overlap on the shared capacity pool. When overlap is unavoidable — for example, ad-hoc analyst queries running during pipeline execution — NordGrid monitors the capacity metrics app for throttling events and adjusts pipeline schedules seasonally. During heavy reporting months (quarter-end), the monthly billing Power BI refresh is moved from 03:00 to 05:00 UTC to give the data engineering pipelines uncontested capacity during their execution window.
V-Order Optimization

V-Order is a Fabric-specific write optimization that sorts data within Parquet files for maximum compression and read performance. When enabled, Delta table writes take slightly longer (5–10% overhead) but the resulting files are smaller and read faster — both by Spark queries and by the SQL analytics endpoint. NordGrid enables V-Order for all Silver and Gold table writes because the tables are written once daily but read dozens of times by analysts, dashboards, and downstream notebooks. The write overhead is amortized across many reads.

# Enable V-Order for Delta table writes
# V-Order sorts data for optimal compression and read performance
# Write takes 5-10% longer, but every subsequent read is faster
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.parquet.vorder.enabled", "true")

# Also enable optimize-write to auto-coalesce small output files
spark.conf.set("spark.microsoft.delta.optimizeWrite.enabled", "true")

df_final.write \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .partitionBy("year", "month") \
    .saveAsTable("silver_meters")

# Result: ~8% slower write, ~25% faster reads for SQL endpoint queries
# and ~15% better compression (smaller files in OneLake)
Session Pool and Warm Starts

Fabric's Spark session cold start (30–60 seconds) is a fixed overhead that cannot be eliminated but can be reduced. Fabric supports session pooling: pre-warmed Spark sessions that are ready to execute code immediately. When session pooling is enabled in the workspace settings, the first cell execution attaches to a pre-warmed session instead of starting a new one, reducing startup from 45 seconds to under 5 seconds. NordGrid enables session pooling for production workspaces because the 40-second savings per pipeline run accumulates to 200+ minutes per week across their 30+ daily notebook runs.
OneLake Caching

When a Spark job reads from OneLake, the data is cached on the local SSD of the executor node. Subsequent reads of the same data are served from the local cache rather than from OneLake, which is significantly faster. This caching behavior benefits notebooks that read the same table multiple times — NordGrid's Silver notebook reads the Silver table once for transformation and once for validation. The validation read is faster because the data is already cached from the transformation read. However, the cache is per-session and per-executor — a new session or a different executor reads from OneLake again.

blocks.title: Fabric performance is not just Spark performance
blocks.content: In a standalone Spark cluster, performance depends only on Spark configuration and data characteristics. In Fabric, performance depends on those factors plus capacity utilization, workload scheduling, V-Order settings, session pool configuration, and OneLake caching behavior. A notebook that runs in 14 minutes during off-peak hours might take 25 minutes during peak hours because of capacity contention. NordGrid treats Fabric performance as a system concern, not just a Spark concern — and the capacity management module (Module 8) covers the platform-level aspects in depth.

The previous nine lessons covered the tools and techniques for Spark performance in Fabric: execution plans, partition pruning, column pruning, partition count tuning, the Spark UI, AQE, diagnostic workflows, and Fabric-specific patterns. This lesson synthesizes everything into NordGrid's production performance governance — the standards, checklists, and processes that ensure performance is maintained as the pipeline evolves, the data grows, and new engineers join the team. Performance governance is not bureaucracy — it is the mechanism that prevents gradual degradation from eventually breaching SLAs.
SLA Definitions

Headroom is the difference between the SLA and the current performance, expressed as a percentage of the SLA. NordGrid maintains a minimum 20% headroom on all pipelines. When headroom drops below 20% — either because data volume grew or because a code change regressed performance — the pipeline is flagged for optimization. The headroom policy prevents SLA breaches by catching gradual degradation before it crosses the threshold. NordGrid's Silver notebook had 50% headroom at launch (10 minutes vs 20-minute SLA) and gradually consumed it over 6 months as data volume grew. The team optimized when headroom reached 15%.
The Plan Review Checklist

items: PartitionFilters present and correct. Every query that filters by time must show year and month in PartitionFilters. An empty PartitionFilters on a time-filtered query is an automatic rejection.

ReadSchema contains only required columns. Compare the ReadSchema to the output schema. If ReadSchema contains columns that do not appear in the final output, add a select() before the expensive operation.
Join strategy appropriate. Small tables (< 10 MB after filters) should use BroadcastHashJoin. Large tables should use SortMergeJoin. If a small table is using SortMergeJoin, add a broadcast() hint.

Shuffle partition count appropriate. Check spark.sql.shuffle.partitions against the expected output volume. The count should produce 128–256 MB partitions for large outputs, or match the expected group count for small aggregations.

No unexpected full table scans. Every FileScan should have either PartitionFilters or PushedFilters or both. A FileScan with empty filters on a query that has WHERE conditions indicates a pushdown failure.

blocks.content: The Performance Review Process

NordGrid's code review checklist includes a performance section. Before any notebook is promoted from staging to production, the reviewing engineer must verify: the execution plan passes the five-point checklist above, the Spark UI shows no skew (max/median task duration < 5x), the total duration is within the SLA with at least 20% headroom, and the capacity consumption is within the notebook's CU budget. The reviewer does not need to optimize the notebook — they need to verify that the author has already optimized it and documented the baseline metrics.
What Comes Next

Module 2 takes the Delta Lake foundation from the junior-level track and adds the mid-level operations that production requires: MERGE for incremental updates, OPTIMIZE and VACUUM for table maintenance, schema evolution for handling upstream changes, and the interaction between Delta operations and the SQL endpoint and semantic model. The performance skills from this module — reading plans, checking pruning, verifying partition counts — apply directly to Delta operations, where a poorly maintained table (fragmented files, stale statistics) degrades query performance over time.

blocks.title: Performance is a practice, not a project
blocks.content: NordGrid does not optimize once and declare victory. Performance degrades gradually as data grows, schemas evolve, and new queries are added. The five-point checklist runs on every code review. The SLA headroom is tracked monthly. The Spark UI is reviewed after every overnight run. Performance is a continuous practice — like testing and deployment — not a one-time project. The team that treats performance as ongoing maintenance keeps their pipelines healthy. The team that treats it as a project discovers SLA breaches at the worst possible moment.
